BOC Life CEO Wilson Tang said there was strong demand for retirement products as a result of the ageing population. People aged 65 and above accounted for 22 per cent of Hong Kong’s 7.5 million residents last year, which would rise to 31 per cent by 2036, according to an official forecast.
“The silver economy is providing new business opportunities for insurers,” Tang said. “BOC Life has provided retirement products to meet the needs of customers who want to retire and live in the Greater Bay Area and other mainland cities.”

data.title: Developing|Hong Kong CityU chief Freddy Boey quits post 2 years earlier than expected
data.description:
The head of Hong Kong’s City University, Professor Freddy Boey Yin Chiang, has resigned with immediate effect for personal reasons, leaving his post two years earlier than expected.
The university expressed its gratitude to Boey, a Singaporean, for his contributions to CityU during his three years as president.
“The executive committee of the [university’s] council has appointed provost and deputy president Professor Lee Chun-sing as acting president with immediate effect to ensure continued operation. The council will provide full support to ensure stability during the transition,” it said on Friday.
It added that a global recruitment exercise for the next president would be launched shortly.
A source on the council said Boey’s five-year contract was supposed to run until 2028.
Boey, an expert in biomaterials engineering, became CityU’s fifth president in May 2023.
The academic, born in 1956, succeeded Professor Way Kuo, who led CityU for 13 years.
The Education Bureau said it respected Boey’s personal wish and decision and thanked him for his contributions to the university during his tenure.
“The recruitment of the president is part of the university’s institutional autonomy and is taken care of by the CityU Council under the authority conferred on it by the CityU Ordinance,” a bureau spokesman said.
“The government has full confidence in the CityU council in selecting the most suitable candidate for the post based on the university’s long-term interests and development needs.”
More to follow…

data.title: Huawei earmarks US$11.7b for training of autonomous-driving systems
data.description:
Adamant about retaining its runaway lead in supplying smart driving systems in China, Huawei Technologies plans to invest as much as 80 billion yuan (US$11.7 billion) over the next five years to boost computing power essential for training and testing semi-autonomous cars.
The massive capital expenditure would enhance the reliability of cars fitted with the Huawei Qiankun ADS autopilot system, as the Shenzhen-based tech giant looked to expand its customer base, said Jin Yuzhi, CEO of Huawei’s intelligent automotive solutions business unit.
In 2026 alone, Huawei would earmark 18 billion yuan for research and development in autonomous driving, he added.
“Autonomous driving is an industry requiring long-term investment,” Jin said at an event in Beijing on Thursday. “Based on our investment of 18 billion yuan in smart driving this year, we will spend more than the combined expense of all other major [autonomous driving] solution providers.”
Computing power plays a key role in improving self-driving capabilities by processing data collected during training and testing of advanced driver assistance systems (ADASs).
Huawei’s Qiankun ADS platform surpassed 10 billion kilometres of autonomous driving early this month.
Autopilot features powered by the platform are widely used in mainland China. Huawei has partnerships with 25 car brands, and Qiankun has been installed in more than 50 models. A total of 1.7 million vehicles fitted with the system have been delivered to Chinese customers.
Aside from Huawei, only US electric vehicle (EV) maker Tesla claims to have amassed as much real-world data.
Some Chinese ADAS systems have proved superior to Tesla’s Full Self-Driving (FSD) navigation software on mainland roads, according to David Zhang, general secretary of the Shanghai-based International Intelligent Vehicle Engineering Association.
However, the Chinese self-driving technologies are so far limited to the domestic market, and FSD is the front-runner internationally, he added.
Huawei supplies domestic carmakers such as Arcfox and Avatr with automotive chips, lidar (light detection and ranging) sensors and digital technologies that allow cars to be semi-autonomous and linked to the internet.
It ventured into the car industry in 2021 through a partnership with Chongqing-based carmaker Seres. Aito, an EV venture between the two companies, is now one of the bestselling premium EV brands in China.

In mid-2024, Seres agreed to pay 2.5 billion yuan to buy the Aito ­trademark and patents from Huawei, a deal that reflected Huawei’s determination to enhance its role as a supplier of vehicle systems and know-how, rather than an EV maker, according to analysts.
Huawei’s pitch to the automotive industry is that it can help its partners cut development costs, accelerate new model launches and improve efficiency in testing smart-driving systems.
Nearly all major carmakers in China are now actively developing cars with “hands off” self-driving features to gain an edge in the cutthroat market.
In December, Beijing gave the first carmakers the go-ahead to build EVs with level 3 (L3) autonomous driving capability. On an international scale from L0 to L5, an L3 autonomous vehicle can operate independently under predefined conditions, although the driver must remain ready at all times to take control if required.

data.title: Henderson’s winning bid to run harbour event space could boost Central’s access
data.description:
A winning bid involving Henderson Land Development to operate a major Hong Kong harbourfront event space has expanded the developer’s footprint in Central to three prime locations, a situation that experts have said could result in greater accessibility and more visitors from surrounding commercial properties.
Central Grand, a 50:50 joint venture formed by Henderson and concert organiser YW Company – commonly known as “Yiu Wing” – recently won the tender to operate the 3.7-hectare (9.1 acres) Central Harbourfront Event Space for five years starting July 1.
The developer already has a substantial footprint in the city’s core business district.
In addition to managing the International Financial Centre (IFC), Henderson in 2021 secured the district’s last major plot – a site dubbed “land king” – to go on sale.
The site is being developed into the Central Yards retail and office space, with the first phase expected to open in 2027 and the second to be completed in 2032.
Yiu Wing, meanwhile, manages AXA Wonderland in the West Kowloon Cultural District and AXA Dreamland at Go Park Sai Sha in the New Territories.
Discussing the tendering exercise for the Central Harbourfront Event Space, lawmaker and urban planner Andrew Lam Siu-lo said on Friday that he agreed that the new requirements for bidders would ensure near year-round access, adding that he considered the measures essential.
“If this public area could be connected to the piers and the very important pedestrian footbridge network [among Central’s commercial buildings] … it will be beneficial to the public,” he told the South China Morning Post.

Lam added that the bridges connecting the harbourfront and other tourist spots to the rest of the district were not only functional but could also serve as attractions in themselves.
The lawmaker said the area was also served by multiple ferry routes and an MTR line, in addition to having ample space for visitor coaches to stop.
The area could serve as a tourist hub, but it would need strong attractions at the event space to bring people there, he added.
“It’s a public space after all,” he said, referring to the event space. “In the past, when no events were taking place, it became more of a place that sat idle than somewhere the public could enjoy.”
Lam said he expected synergy and management efficiency as Henderson would be involved in overseeing the event space.
But the lawmaker said he remained cautious as any benefits brought to the area would depend on the approach taken by the joint venture.
“There is the flexibility [for synergy], but it’s down to operational details later on. [Central Yards] will need more time before it’s done … What would the conveniences be? No one knows yet,” he said.
“We can see on paper as to what would be inside … a bridge that is accessible at all times, and it connects with public spaces. But in reality, how would they be perceived by the general public? That is a whole different matter,” he said.

Ivan Ho Man-yiu, the Harbourfront Commission’s chairman, said he was looking forward to the joint venture running the space, given the potential of “the significant synergy”.
He compared the relationship between the event space, the IFC and Central Yards to that of shopping centre K11 Musea and the Avenue of Stars in Tsim Sha Tsui.
But he stressed the importance of making sure events held there were not too commercial and could be enjoyed by all.
“The public’s enjoyment and engagement with the harbourfront are our priorities,” Ho said.
Some clauses of the tender enable the tenant or the Development Bureau to intervene to prevent repeats of controversies such as the hot air balloon festival last September that failed to provide tethered rides as advertised.
“We also require the tenants of the space to communicate regularly, and that they should consult the bureau and the commission on their yearly plans as to the kinds of activities and arrangements they plan to put on,” Ho said.
Ho said that the creation of a new tender clause allowing the government to issue a fine if the number of event days held at the space fell short of commitments was due to the penalty never being clearly laid out in the past.
“But based on past experience, [meeting the required number of event days] should not be difficult to achieve,” he said.
In a statement on Thursday, the joint venture said that it would create synergies and showcase both sides of Victoria Harbour, and would submit detailed operational and execution plans to authorities.
It pledged to open the entire public space on days when there were no commercial events taking place. The venture is also set to pay the government HK$1.51 million (US$192,780) in monthly rent.
